title: 2025 07 31 EDSI (2)
displayTitle: 2025-07-30 - Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee - Review briefing on the 2023/24 annual review of the Real Estate Agents Authority
committee: Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee
topic: Review briefing on the 2023/24 annual review of the Real Estate Agents Authority
description: Review briefing on the 2023/24 annual review of the Real Estate Agents Authority Real Estate Agents Authority Belinda Moffat, Chief Executive/Registrar Denese Bates KC, REA Board Chair
descriptionSummary: Review briefing on the 2023/24 annual review of the Real Estate Agents Authority. Real Estate Agents Authority. Belinda Moffat, Chief Executive/Registrar

chunks.text: To start with some opening remarks, keeping them short, one to two minutes, and then we will, um, have questions for members. Thank you. So Tina, I'm Denise Bates and I'm the chair of the Real Estate Authority Board. I'm here today with Belinda Moffit, uh, r e's Chief Executive.

Uh, and we are going to both be presenting the annual review briefing about 20, 23, 24 year. I've prepared some opening remarks, provide an overview of that year, uh, and to comment on areas that, uh, we, the, that of focus that we have is real estate, uh, real estate conduct regulator.

So this will take about 10 minutes, um, because I think, you know, if you wanna ask questions, we need to leave the room for them. Yeah. So, um, I think, um, if you keep them really short, I would say if there's lots to cover, maybe max like three, two to three minutes. So then you can cover rest in the, in, uh, Answer questions. So you'd like me to Keep your opening remarks brief, so if you take up 10 minutes, there won't be enough time for members, uh, for questions.

So if you keep them to three minutes or so, three or four minutes, that should be sufficient. Um, alright. Thank you. I'll do my best to, uh, to do it that way. So, um, you presumably know that we're a Crown agent established under the act, and that our purpose is to protect the interests of consumers engaging in real estate transactions and to promote public confidence in the profession.

And so we carry out our re regulatory responsibilities in a number of ways. Uh, we, we, we do the licensing work. We oversee the code of conduct. Uh, we provide guidance and education to the profession. Uh, we deal about, we deal with the whole complaint system, uh, so that, uh, we can hold licensees to account for, for, for, uh, for bad behavior essentially.

And lastly, uh, we are there to protect and promote the interests of consumers being a consumer agency, consumer protection agency, as well as a conduct regulator. So, two strengths to what we do. Um, we've got a very good website called Settled, uh, and that, uh, that is a very good tool for, uh, prospective purchases to use.

So we do our best to promote that the year we're talking about 2324. Uh, it was notable for a number of reasons, um, probably don't need to tell you that the economic conditions are challenging, uh, have been since that year. And that slower market puts a lot of pressure on the, a the, uh, agents and the consumers.

So while the residential stocks are increased, the sales are slow. And, uh, we have to, uh, the, the profession has to manage consumer expectations, uh, and that that can be quite an recalling market. So what that's meant for us, actually is a very large increase in the number of complaints that we've had to manage.

So I will give you a few numbers. Uh, 361 complaints, uh, in the, the year we're looking at, uh, compared with 252, uh, in the 22 3 year. And, uh, the, the, the, they're continuing to increase at a pace. Um, we saw in the year we are looking at, uh, cases involving some complex issues.

Uh, so that all goes to slow down the process. So one of our big initiatives has been to, um, review our complaints process, uh, to see if we can streamline it and refine it and become more efficient. And we, uh, have done a, a good job of doing that.

Um, unfortunately, uh, what, what it's, we are coming up against a wall of a huge wall of complaints at the time, same time as we are trying to get through, um, a bit of a backlog. So that's, uh, and I think we are rising to the challenge, but, uh, look, the, the good news is the, we, we know that this complaint environment is probably going to be around, and so we call it the new normal.

And we'll probably, uh, it'll probably change at some point, but right now that we, we are dealing with a, a lot of disappointed people, I'd say. Um, so just to give you a, uh, we, we've, we've actually getting through more complaints and we are also reducing the time it takes to do them.

Uh, so just to give you some figures, um, the, the year we're looking at, we did 3 11 3, 311 complaints. And in the year prior to that, we did 253. And this year we've done 487, um, which I think shows our, uh, increase in efficiencies given the volume keeps coming in.

But, but, but we, we are really focusing on that and, uh, you know, doing workforce planning, whatever we can to keep it down, which is challenging also without, uh, and to do that and live within our budget concerns, uh, without, within our, within our budget constraints, should I say.

So we, we've been fiscally responsible, but we are aware that we have this, uh, this complaint environment, which is quite difficult and, you know, puts pressure on people that are doing it. So you expect, you might expect that that, uh, licensing numbers would reduced a lot in the environment.

Well, in that year, uh, reduced by only 2.9%. Um, and that included 1,710 new licensees joining. And this year, uh, the number has increased again, uh, the overall number to 15,692. And that's despite the challenging conditions. So we've delivered a, we an effective CPD program, and that is, uh, it seems to be from the survey evidence we have, it seems to be, uh,

meeting requirements within the profession. Um, we had a lift, uh, of, to 72% following 67% in the previous year. Um, and, and again, we've got this year's results and it's now gone to 78%. So it's getting towards where we think it will, will, will be.

Um, it should mention that, that, that, that, that there is a judicial review of our decision to mandate A CPD copy on understanding the needs and interests of Maori called, yeah. Uh, the, the hearing was June, 2024. We got a, a positive decision from the high court, but it's under, under appeal, so really can't say too much more, um, thank you.

About the court process. Yeah. Thank you. So, um, it looks like you have prepared quite a bit to present to the committee. Is it possible for you to provide, um, that yeah, that presentation to the committee so that then committee can read through and can include any questions in the post hearing in Christians so that we can start with Christians now?

Yeah, I'm sorry that I wasn't able to, um, to, to do it all as quickly as you. No, no, no, no. I think you've covered quite a bit, so, and this will be useful for questions. Thank you very much. That's very good. Thank you members. Um, please, um, Thank you. Um, thank you for, um, your time today.

Look, I wanna pick up on the, um, complaints process. Yes. You've just referred to, um, and, you know, commend you on the, the speed resolution for some of those complaints. Yeah. Um, but you don't wanna necessarily close the door completely right. And turn people off from a process just because you wanna have, have a quick resolution, that's not the right thing to do on in all circumstances.

No. So what sort of metrics do you have around the timeliness of decision making and acknowledging that, um, sometimes, you know, like matters before you just being tribunal, for example, is a little bit more advanced than a, a complaints assessment committee process. So what metrics around timeliness for Resolution? Well, I'll start that one and hand over to Belinda.

Uh, so at the moment, if you, if it goes to a CAC, then the average time is 110 days. And if it, uh, um, if it doesn't now Belinda, you can take that. I think, Yeah, we, our aim is to, the registrar has certain powers to triage and or resolve a complaint, so we try to use those powers within three months of receiving the complaint because that can require a resolution process, making sure we received information.

Sure. If it then goes to a CAC, our aspiration is for it to be dealt with within nine months. And that's the target. We are not, we are not achieving yet. Uh, and at the, and so the pre CAC times have come down from about 150 days to now about 115. Um, our CA, if a case goes to the CAC or, um, on average, it is still taking over a year.

And that is what we are really working hard to bring down. Um, I would add that in this period we were having quite a big turnover of our CAC members, so we were having to upskill a new panel. Um, they've all worked really hard and we working hard with the CAC members to support them to determine these cases more swiftly. Equally, we're asking parties to be more responsive to their submission obligations to work within timelines.

Uh, but obviously natural justice is very important. So it's a balance. Yes. Well, well, the, the legislative process that we have to follow under the act is, uh, doesn't result in particularly strict conclusions. It's quite a thorough process with every CAC having three members on, and those three members having to do an investigation and come to a decision.

So, and, and, and there's lots of toing and froing. Now we are doing our best to refine it. We have some ideas to, for dealing with that in, in different ways. But, but that's in process. I should add that the systems upgrade, um, I, I think will, will, will make a step change. Mm-hmm.

And so, can I respond just briefly? You did ask for metrics, and I was just checking my notes. We did, in the 2024 year, we completed 81% of the formal complaints across all categories, um, within a year. So can I just follow up briefly? Thank you. Um, have you thought though about formalizing that sort of measurement on an annual basis so that you might, you might have aspirational, you know, sort of desires to hit a particular target, but, and appreciate that there might be some systemic changes happening, but to what extent are you looking

to formalize some of those changes? So our statement of performance expectations, in fact over many years and including this year, does have a tar the targets to complete complaints before the CAC process within three months to complete the CAC process within nine months. And those are the targets we are working towards and we've published, um, and people are holding us to account for that.

Yeah, so, so, so just to summarize, we've got the legislative process. We can't do very much about what would be hard to, um, so we've got the systems, processes and systems that we use to deal with it as sufficiently as possible. And then there are, is the people who are actually on the ground doing it and making sure we have enough resource there.

So that's, that's probably summarizes it. Mm-hmm. A atory from Dr. Ham. Thank you. Um, there's been an increase of 43%, um, of, of complaints. What's, what's actually drive driving that? I'll start, start that one off. Um, we, we think it is a market conditions, uh, that, that are in play at the moment because, uh, we've got a lot of sellers who don't like the fact that the prices have tumbled.

And in Auckland they've tumbled quite a lot. Hmm. Uh, so that, and then, so, and you've got, and then you've got the agent trying to manage that expectation. And I think that it's a sort of recipe for something to not go wrong unless it's very careful and considered management and manage the expectations.

And I would say as well, Mr. Campbell, that, um, there is more awareness of REA and that we are available to receive people's concerns. Um, as I said, we are, we have seen quite a big increase in lower level concerns, which we use in triage powers to deal with, which can be disappointing for some of our consumers, which we, we acknowledge, but we have to use the system effectively.

I think overall there is also just generally a consumer sentiment. People are wanting to raise their concerns and, uh, we are seeing it across other, um, conduct regulators and dispute resolution agencies. Um, so there's a general increase in, in complaints. Um, and what we have noticed in particular is just a less, less willingness to want to resolve.

People want to see a determination and an outcome. So that's why the formal complaint has come to us to, just to add to that 1, 1 1, 1, 1 thing we are doing with the sector is we are saying to them it's, it is a tough market. Consumers need great customer service, so we're asking them to use their internal complaints systems well to try to resolve put right things where they can so that we are focusing on the most important issues.

Yes. Because I should just add that there's been this, this is a reluctance often to use the, the, the in-house complaints resolution system and come straight to REA and in many of cases I think they could be dealt with in house. So, and you might have just touched on, um, and already, um, and I will know there's, what about 96,000 real estate sales a year?

Complaints are, what, 361 in the period? So it is, there's a small percentage Yes. Of, of, um, things, uh, are you taking any proactive steps to try and reduce complaints actually getting to, to actually that level or any kind of, uh, preventative steps to kind of prevent Yeah.

People having these complaints? Can I, Yes. Um, we have a, we, we, we are, we, we have a two pronged approach at REA. Because of our statute, we are here to lift confidence in the sector, which means we have to work really hard with the, the sector to help them know what their, what their obligations are. And we are here to help consumers.

So we provide consumers with a lot of information through settled because the transaction is complex. And often, and most of our complaints are in residential and many people only encounter a transaction once in their lifetime or maybe every 10 years. So we support the consumer to understand the system and set all our website's key for that.

The second one is our education program. The information we give to our sector through our newsletters, uh, through our website, helps 'em understand what are the most important issues. We publish the complaint themes, we say, this is where you really need to focus. And we have a really strong engagement program with our sector, uh, to meet with our sec the sector leaders to say, this is what we are seeing, this is what we need you to focus on in your agencies to ensure that the issues that are the subject of complaint have been dealt with.

So it's a two-prong working with consumers and the sector. Mm-hmm. Uh, uh, so, uh, from me on that, uh, is there a breakdown of, um, nature of complaints, uh, available? Uh, we, we can make it available in terms of the percentage across the themes in our annual report, we pub published the top five, uh, themes.

Yeah. Um, we haven't put as scribes it according to, um, the percentage that relates to it, but they certainly are the top five, um, complaint themes that we can provide that information of the, the, um, thank you committee would like that To stand it for the sake of completeness that, uh, lawyers don't tend to get involved until there's a signed agreement.

And, uh, by that time, uh, if there's gonna be damage, it's the time timely damage will have been done by. Um, so that's the sort of focus that, uh, is that time. Mm-hmm. Alright. Uh, you from Georgia Cord and, um, thank you for allowing me to join Madam Chair through, um, the hearing.

Um, I'm interested in weather tightness issues and those issues that have been in the media recently around, um, lack of disclosure by an agent. I wanted to ask you about whether you have explored changes in the rules, um, that apply to your organization, um, to bring, um, either discipline action or to allow guidance for situations like in the media now where a salesperson has been required under rule 10.7 to, um, make available information that's disclosed to them about whether tightness drop the listing because they were instructed not to pass that on

to potential buyers. And then another sales person has picked that up. That seems like a very clear lacuna here, um, where we are allowing salespeople to take on, um, listings where others don't. And so in the interest of your organization and preserving, um, observ the, the professional conduct of those people who don't take on listings where they see an issue, um, to then be under others, It is one of the biggest challenges and one of the biggest frustrations for the sector to see that when you are a licensee who is instructed not

to disclose a defect, which is an obligation as you said, but we say you have to walk away because you can't mislead the consumer. And so when they see it, uh, the same property being, uh, uh, sale marketed down the road and they don't see that disclosure they have, that is a misconduct issue and under our rules, they ought to be reporting that to us.

So that, so that is where the system is intended to catch that. Um, it doesn't happen and it doesn't happen every time. Sometimes we do get reports that, uh, another agency is promoting a property that is clearly, um, does have clearly have weather tightness issues. The difficulty is when there's a hidden defect because the disclosure of if the, if the second sales person has not been given the information and the vendor has withheld it, uh, then there, then there is a challenge for a potential buyer.

But then the potential buyer, which is what we do with the buyers, is to say, you must do your due diligence. You must get a voting report. You should look at the limb, you should make sure you've done a very good inquiry. Um, and then there are obviously some warranties under the sale and purchase agreement, but the reporting requirement is where we try to catch that, that I guess what you'd call a lacuna.

Yeah. And it is a but it is, it is a challenge. And as I say, we would say that's a conduct issue. If someone picks up a listing and doesn't, doesn't disclose a weather, an obvious weather tightness issue, then that is a conduct issue that we would like to think would come to us. I guess what I'm asking is, yes, there is a contact issue there, but we know that, um, the, you know, prosecutions under this, not prosecutions, but disciplinary issues under, under these sorts of rules are few and far between.

And so, um, another way to deal with this would be the organization advocating for a change which required proactive disclosure rather than making it an offense to fail to disclose. Um, have you explored, um, whether that would deal with this issue? Because I imagine that in the case that I'm talking about, it's a well-publicized case.

The Bailey's agent who dropped the listing and then the Barefoots agent that picked it up, there's some disquiet amongst the industry between those sellers who will drop the listing and then the sellers who will pick It up. So I can't look, it's really difficult and I have to be very careful. Can't, we can't comment on the specifics of that case. Um, obviously it's what these matters can do come to us.

I mean there the rules do have a, a proactive disclosure obligation. The rules expect that a, um, that a, that a licensee must know the property and must disclose any defects which are reasonably competent licensee would identify, um, or they must disclose information which in fairness to the parties ought to be disclosed.

So there are these two conduct obligations that do exist. Yes. Um, but I, I, I take, I acknowledge your question and I'd like to consider that further with our, our team, um, and to, to look at whether there is any additional guidance we can be, we can be providing to the sector. Yeah. Well you de essentially dealing with the set behavior on the part of the seller and uh, but, but it does lead on, as you say, that we, we can't control what, we can't control what the seller does. Okay.

Alright. Okay. Um, Scott will, Yeah. Yeah. Chair. Um, so you've talked about the, the new normal of complaints mm-hmm. The complaint with, I'm, I'm wondering what you think this might, where, where it sits in what you think this might result from, sort of seems like a bit of a lack of civility in society, but you know, I mean if, if are, are there, are there complaints about cowboy activities or are they, are they, are they justified or you know, in general, can you give us a sense or are they stresses that might be causing it?

I I mean I'm, I'm interested. We have thought we have, we have thought about that quite a lot and Belinda has spoken about it a lot. So would you like to take, One of the points that Denise and I talked about that Denise was to make was that while we've seen this substantial increase in complaints, we've actually ly seen a reduction in the findings of misconduct.

So we've had in the past 20%, then it fell to 16%. And as we become aware of more the conduct issues we are finding is less, which I, I think we would say that's the regulatory system working as it ought to, is that most of our sector is really working hard to meet the standards, but there are still some that are not.

So we are, our role is still very important. Um, so I think that is, you know, that is one of the, that is, that is one of the trends that, that we don't have. We think the regulatory system that was put in place in 2008 has really made a, an important impact. Um, and that our most of our sector is working really hard. Um, they say we, we are not seeing a, a, a significant increase in very, very bad behavior, but there is still some, and that's where we have to act.

Mm-hmm. And that's why we have to clear that, make sure we, we we triage the cases to focus on those most cases. Well, Well there is, and I don't have the percentage, uh, and it might be available, but a, a lot, a lot of the cases are, um, there's really essentially a finding that, um, there's not, not enough to warrant a complaint.

Uh, and I think that that, that in itself, uh, creates tension. Um, many people think they've got a valid complaint, they haven't, not enough to justify going through the system. And, uh, I just get back to, uh, to how, how real estate professionals to try to deal with this.

And that is by carefully managing expectations in the first place and, and communicate and communicating. Belinda mentioned giving, you know, this, this, this environment requires professionals to give really good customer service. That's the key.

So I guess what the figures don't tell us is that they're more complex situation where there might be an improvement in behavior and, and that you are seeing in the sector, but also possibly a lag and a less tolerant, less tolerant consumers who, who expect things to be better and possibly a perception of a, a poor perception of, of the sector that leads them to expect that that outcome.

Is that something that, I mean that that if you're talking about this mismatch and, and you're seeing improved outcomes, but an increase in complaints that are not actually Justified or Yeah, that's, That's in, in our measures, we do report, one of our measures is to report the percentage of misconduct findings.

So it is an SPE measure to the point I was asked before. So it is a, it is a measure that we report. Um, but I acknowledge your, your observation that may be our report could demonstrate more because we do, we do often, um, what in, in our annual report, we do report on the complaint outcomes and we do report on, for example, the number of misconduct cases taken to the tribunal.

So people will see the licensing numbers, the 15,000, and then they'll see those complaint outcomes from tribunals being quite small, that if your observation is, we could tell that performance story more in our in report. That's, that's a useful observation. Um, in terms of a less tolerant consumer, it's interesting.

I, I would like to think that the consumer has a, as a voice and that our system is enabling that voice to be heard. Because as Denise said, the fact that it may not justify regulatory intervention or a misconduct finding doesn't mean that a consumer doesn't feel that a better job could have been done. Mm-hmm. And it doesn't mean that they didn't receive the best information in a timely way.

Mm-hmm. So often a lot of the work that, um, my head of regulatory services, who's with me today, his team worked really hard to do, is to communicate a lot of information to our consumers. And so at an early stage they are given bit quite a lot of guidance because Denise said it's often after the event. Um, alright. Um, any question from Dr. De?

Just, just one and sorry that we keep focusing on, um, thanks, but jump up. It's one of our major tasks. Yeah, I did. Um, simple question. Um, could you make a comment around trust in the process from the people from the complainants Trust in the process, In the process?

So when they get to the end of it, and there may be people that are aggre, there may be others that go, that's fair. So what sort of trust do people have? I think there's some survey evidence around That. Yeah, so look, we do, um, and we can just turn to those pages in, in the notes, but we, we are pleased that overall consumer and licensee, but focus on the consumer.

'cause it is hard to be told we are not gonna take your case forward. And we do work really hard to, to try and do that in an empathetic way. Unfortunately, our act gives us the power to close a cons, a complaint when the act says it's inconsequential. We don't like that word because it's not inconsequential to that person. Yeah. So we do try to explain whether it's because we don't have evidence or because it's not sufficiently serious we do that.

But, um, consumers, our survey for this year that we were looking at said that 76% of consumers have confidence that the real estate industry is well regulated. And we think that's a pretty positive result. And those are people who have bought or sold in the 12 months. Um, 87, 80 7% of consumers feel empowered to participate effectively in real estate transactions.

And we have to say those who've seen our settled website, the number goes even higher. Um, 92% of licensees, um, in our, in our licensee survey found that the compliance guidance we give is useful. And 74% of the sector, uh, thinks we are well regulated. And I'm sure that there would be no regulator that would have a, a very high number of their regulated population.

But we think those are reasonably good. Good confidence results. I Think we should just say about supervision. Yes. So I just, um, conferring, uh, I think, I think the, um, we are trying to emphasize the importance of supervision in the whole process and the, and by the firms that they've got proper supervision in there.

Uh, 'cause that's monitoring the quality control, which is also monitoring the sort of behavior set that are, um, likely to happen. And I think that those supervisors provide the leadership and knowledge and guidances so, so very much needed by those who are starting out.

So there were sort of a supplementary, so We'll have to stop there. So I think, uh, thank you so much, Denise. Thank you Belinda. And if you, uh, are okay to provide copy of your presentation that you want, um, due to the committee, that would be really helpful and there might be some, uh, questions, uh, sent pledge on as well To you. Thank you. So thank you very much for, for having us, uh, here today.
It's much Thank You. You very much.
